channel.title: From the Mythology Notes — George Alexander Vela
channel.description: Essays on Greek mythology by George Alexander Vela.

item.title: Hector: The Man Who Knew He Was Going to Lose
item.description: Hector is the best man in Troy and he knows the city will fall. The Iliad makes him fight anyway — and in doing so creates the ancient world's most honest portrait of courage.

item.title: Jason and Medea: A Love Story That Ends in Ruins
item.description: Jason and Medea is the ancient world's most devastating love story — not because it ends in tragedy but because Euripides shows us exactly how it got there, one rational decision at a time.

item.title: The Trojan Horse: What Homer Actually Said
item.description: Homer never describes the Trojan Horse in detail. The story everyone knows comes from Virgil, from Quintus of Smyrna, from sources centuries after the Iliad. The original version is stranger.

item.title: Circe: The Witch the Odyssey Was Afraid Of
item.description: Circe turns men into pigs and lives alone on an island. The Odyssey treats her as a threat to be neutralized. But read carefully, and she is the most self-possessed figure in the poem.

item.title: Achilles and Patroclus: The Friendship the Iliad Cannot Quite Name
item.description: Homer never names what Achilles and Patroclus are to each other. The ancient world argued about it for centuries. The argument itself is the most honest reading of the Iliad available.

item.title: Theseus and the Minotaur: Who Was the Real Monster?
item.description: The Minotaur was born of divine punishment and confined to a labyrinth he did not build. Theseus was celebrated for killing him. The myth rewards a second look at who it asks you to root for.

item.title: Orpheus and Eurydice: Why He Looked Back
item.description: Orpheus descended into the underworld to retrieve his dead wife and was told not to look back. He looked back. The myth never explains why — and that silence is the whole point.

item.title: Clytemnestra: The Villain the Myths Needed You to Hate
item.description: Clytemnestra killed her husband and is remembered as a monster. But Aeschylus gives her a case worth hearing — and the Oresteia is most interesting when you realize she is not wrong.

item.title: Cassandra: The Curse of Being Right About Everything
item.description: Apollo gave Cassandra the gift of prophecy, then cursed her so no one would believe her. But the myth is stranger than that summary suggests — and more honest about what it means to know the future.

item.title: Medusa: The Monster Who Was Made, Not Born
item.description: Medusa was not always a monster. Ovid tells us who she was before Perseus arrived — and what Athena's punishment reveals about how mythology handles victims it cannot afford to sympathize with.

item.title: Hecuba of Troy: The Queen Behind the War
item.description: Hecuba was the most competent person in any room she entered. The myth of the Trojan War is full of gods and heroes; she ran the city that made the war possible for ten years.

item.title: The Real Myth of Bellerophon: Why He Fell From Heaven
item.description: Bellerophon tamed Pegasus, killed the Chimera, and survived three attempts to kill him. The myth everyone remembers is the fall. The story worth reading is everything that came before it.

item.title: Who Was Penthesilea? The Amazon Queen History Almost Forgot
item.description: Her name appears in Homer's Iliad exactly once. What the summaries of the lost Aethiopis tell us about the Amazon queen who came to Troy knowing she would die — and came anyway.
